title: Death rate by cause
subtitle: The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and [non-communicable diseases](#dod:non-communicable-diseases).
caption:
Death rate by cause, World
The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and non-communicable diseases.

This stacked area chart tracks the global estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people across three broad cause categories from 1980 to 2023, using an age-standardized metric to allow for comparisons over time. The vertical axis measures the death rate from 0 to 1,400, while the horizontal axis spans the 43-year period. The total height of the stacked areas shows a consistent downward trajectory, falling from a combined rate of approximately 1,300 in 1980 to roughly 750 in 2023. The bottom layer, representing non-communicable diseases (NCDs), begins near 800 in 1980 and declines gradually to just above 500 by the end of the period, remaining the largest single component throughout. The middle green band, covering communicable, maternal, neonatal, and nutritional diseases, starts at a thickness of roughly 400 units and contracts significantly over the decades, shrinking to a band of approximately 200 units by 2023. The top tan layer, representing injuries, maintains a relatively stable thickness of roughly 100 to 150 units across the entire timeline. A distinct deviation from the long-term decline occurs around 2020, where the total rate spikes upward briefly before resuming its downward path toward 2023. The data shows that while death rates from all three categories have decreased since 1980, the reduction in communicable diseases has been the most pronounced in terms of absolute magnitude lost from the total.
